article_title: NASA's Juno Catches Jupiter's Bow Shock Accelerating Electrons to Near-Light Speed, Yielding a Scaling Law for Cosmic Ray Origins

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)
Source redirected to different domain: idp.nature.com

content_preview.title: NASA's Juno Catches Jupiter's Bow Shock Accelerating Electrons to Near-Light Speed, Yielding a Scaling Law for Cosmic Ray Origins
content_preview.summary: Juno detected electrons reaching 1 MeV in Jupiter's foreshock, and the authors derive a scaling law linking shock size to maximum particle energy.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA's Juno spacecraft has detected electrons accelerated to nearly the speed of light at Jupiter's bow shock, and the team behind the measurement used the data to propose a scaling law that links the size of a shock to the maximum energy it can impart to particles. According to [NASA Science](https://science.nasa.gov/blogs/science-news/2026/06/03/nasas-juno-reveals-new-insights-into-cosmic-ray-origins/), the particles "traveling close to the speed of light near Jupiter were capture...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (583 words, within the 400-1200 range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Why It Matters arc. Appropriate technical depth on foreshock transients, the Hillas limit, and the cross-environment energy scaling, without overstating the result.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk and verified. source-0 (NASA Science, 200): confirms the verbatim 'traveling close to the speed of light near Jupiter were captured by NASA's Juno mission...' quote, the June 3 2026 Nature publication, the 'measured high-speed electrons in Jupiter's foreshock region' and 'reached even higher speeds than Earth's, scaling with the giant planet's larger-sized bow shock' quotes, and the 'this scaling relationship matched cosmic rays seen coming from supernovas across the galaxy...' quote. source-1 (Phys.org, Archive.org fallback, 200): confirms Raptis-led analysis of Juno data, the verbatim 'within this foreshock, transient plasma structures accelerated particles to relativistic speeds', 'the size of such foreshocks scales with the overall size of a shock system and sets a practical upper limit on the achievable particle energy', 'derive a relationship between foreshock transient size and maximum particle energies', the 'extending the results to distant astrophysical shocks requires assumptions beyond direct measurement...' caveat, plus the paper title and DOI 10.1038/s41586-026-10473-z. source-2 (Sci.News, 200): confirms Raptis's Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory affiliation, the October 1 2023 observation date, electrons up to 1 MeV, the verbatim 'universal scaling law for the Hillas limit that empirically connects the observable size of a transient to maximum particle energy', the MeV/tens-of-GeV/tens-of-TeV energies across planetary bow shocks / protostellar jets / supernova remnants, and the full formal citation 'Nature 654, 47-51; doi: 10.1038/s41586-026-10473-z'. source-3 (Nature, 3036 bytes): a bot-block 'Client Challenge' page with no article content (redirected to idp.nature.com), as anticipated.
editor_notes.rule9_assessment: Rule 9 scrutiny applied. The primary Nature paper (source-3) is auth-walled and its snapshot is a JavaScript challenge page carrying no article text. The Nature URL is cited only for bibliographic specifics (title, volume 654, pages 47-51, DOI). Every one of those load-bearing specifics is independently verifiable in non-blocked sources: the title and DOI appear in both Phys.org and Sci.News, and the complete citation including volume 654 and pages 47-51 appears verbatim in the Sci.News snapshot ('Nature 654, 47-51; doi: 10.1038/s41586-026-10473-z'). No factual claim rests on the blocked Nature page. Rule 9: PASS.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote, number (1 MeV, tens of GeV, tens of TeV, volume 654, pages 47-51, Oct 1 2023, June 3 2026), name (Savvas Raptis, JHU APL), and the Hillas-limit framing traces to a cited, non-blocked source. The summary's two claims (1 MeV in the foreshock; scaling law linking shock size to maximum particle energy) are both supported. No hallucinations detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Hedging is appropriately preserved (the 'What We Don't Know' section foregrounds that extending to distant shocks is an inference, mirroring the authors' own caveat). No sensationalism, no AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Original. The only recent cosmic-ray article (2026-06-13, Amaterasu particle) concerns the chemical composition of ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays in a Penn State / Physical Review Letters study — a distinct topic, study, mechanism, and finding. No duplication.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully sourced submission. All four sources read and verified; the auth-walled Nature paper is cited only for bibliographic data that is independently confirmed in accessible sources (Rule 9 PASS). Approved for publication as-is.
